enrichment.short_description: Open-source dashboard platform for connecting databases, APIs, and analytics tools into live KPI charts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Chartbrew is an open-source dashboard tool that connects to databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB), APIs, and services like Stripe, Google Analytics, Firestore, Supabase, and Airtable to turn raw data into charts and dashboards.
It is built and maintained by Depomo Ltd, a UK-based team, and is available both as a hosted cloud product and as a self-hosted deployment via Docker for teams that want full control over their data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Chartbrew targets product teams, SaaS companies, and agencies that need shareable, live KPI dashboards without setting up a full business intelligence stack.

features.title: Multi-source connections
features.description: Connect PostgreSQL, MongoDB, MySQL, Stripe, Google Analytics, Firestore, Supabase, Airtable, and more as data sources.

features.title: Reusable datasets
features.description: Build a dataset once and reuse it across multiple charts and dashboards.

features.title: AI-assisted queries
features.description: Built-in AI assistant to help write SQL and build queries for exploring connected data.

features.title: Automated refresh and alerts
features.description: Schedule data refreshes, send dashboard snapshots, and trigger alerts through Slack, email, or webhooks.

features.title: Flexible sharing
features.description: Share dashboards internally, embed charts in products, or grant controlled client access.

features.title: Self-hosting
features.description: Deploy Chartbrew on your own infrastructure using the open-source project for complete data control.

pros: Open-source with a free self-hosted option
Connects to a wide range of databases and business tools out of the box
Dashboard sharing and embedding built in
Automated alerts reduce manual monitoring
cons: Hosted cloud plans add up quickly for larger teams (Professional plan is $249/month)
AI features are metered by monthly credits on paid plans
Self-hosting requires Docker and ongoing maintenance

faqs.question: Is Chartbrew free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Chartbrew project is open source and can be self-hosted for free. Chartbrew also offers a hosted cloud version with paid plans starting at $24/month.

faqs.question: Can Chartbrew connect to my database directly?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chartbrew supports direct connections to PostgreSQL, MySQL, and MongoDB, along with API-based connections to services like Stripe and Google Analytics.

faqs.question: Does Chartbrew support team collaboration?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid plans include multiple team members, with limits ranging from 3 members on the Starter plan to unlimited members on the Professional plan.

use_cases.title: Client reporting for agencies
use_cases.description: Agencies build client-facing dashboards pulling from ad platforms and databases to report campaign or project metrics.

use_cases.title: SaaS product metrics
use_cases.description: Product teams connect Stripe and their app database to track revenue, churn, and usage in one dashboard.

features: Unlimited dashboards on your own infrastructure

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chartbrew is an open-source dashboard tool that connects databases and APIs into live, shareable KPI charts. Free to self-host or use hosted.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source database schema visualization tool that turns SQL databases into interactive ER diagrams instantly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ChartDB is a database schema visualization and data-modeling tool that generates entity-relationship (ER) diagrams from a database with a single query, without requiring persistent database access.

It supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, SQL Server, SQLite, ClickHouse, CockroachDB, Oracle, Snowflake, and BigQuery, and is available as free self-hosted open-source software or as a hosted cloud product with paid tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: ChartDB is aimed at engineering teams, database architects, and technical leads who need to document, understand, or communicate the structure of complex or unfamiliar databases.

features.title: Instant schema import
features.description: Generate a full ER diagram from a live database in seconds using a single read-only query.

features.title: Broad database support
features.description: Works with PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, SQL Server, SQLite, ClickHouse, CockroachDB, Oracle, Snowflake, and BigQuery.

features.title: AI-assisted diagramming
features.description: Generate or adjust ER diagrams from natural-language prompts using the built-in AI assistant (paid tiers).

features.title: SQL export
features.description: Export the visualized schema as SQL in multiple database dialects.

features.title: Real-time collaboration
features.description: Multiple team members can view and edit the same diagram together on Teams plans.

features.title: Version history
features.description: Track schema changes over time with change logs and versioning on paid plans.

pros: Free, open-source self-hosted version with no artificial limits on the core diagramming feature
Supports a wide range of relational and cloud data warehouses
Very fast schema import compared to manually drawing ER diagrams
SQL export makes diagrams actionable, not just visual
cons: Free cloud plan is limited to 1 database and 10 tables
Real-time collaboration and AI assistant require paid Teams plan
Advanced features like SSO and audit logs require custom/enterprise pricing

faqs.question: Is ChartDB free?
faqs.answer: Yes, ChartDB has a free self-hosted open-source version and a free cloud tier limited to 1 database with up to 10 tables. Paid Pro and Teams plans remove those limits and add collaboration features.

faqs.question: Does ChartDB need direct access to my database?
faqs.answer: No persistent access is required; ChartDB imports the schema with a single query and does not need a standing connection to generate the diagram.

faqs.question: Can I export the diagram as SQL?
faqs.answer: Yes, ChartDB can export the visualized schema as SQL in multiple database dialects.

use_cases.title: Onboarding to an unfamiliar database
use_cases.description: Engineers new to a codebase generate an ER diagram to quickly understand table relationships.

use_cases.title: Documenting schema for a team
use_cases.description: Database architects embed diagrams in internal documentation and keep them synced with schema changes.

pricing_plans.billing_cycle: monthly or annual (up to 40% discount annually)
monthly or annual (up to 40% discount annually)

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ChartDB turns SQL databases into ER diagrams in seconds. Free open-source self-hosted tool with optional paid cloud plans for teams.

enrichment.short_description: Web-based database manager from the DBeaver team, letting teams browse and query databases from a browser.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CloudBeaver is a lightweight, browser-based database management tool built on the DBeaver platform. It brings a subset of DBeaver's SQL client capabilities to a web interface so teams can browse, query, and manage databases without installing desktop software.

The Community Edition is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, self-hosted via Docker. DBeaver Corporation also sells a CloudBeaver Enterprise edition with SSO (AWS SSO, GCP SSO, Okta), Kerberos, and multi-factor authentication for organizations, priced at $205 per user per year.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: CloudBeaver suits teams that want shared, browser-accessible database access without distributing desktop installs — from individual developers running it locally to organizations that need centralized, access-controlled database management.

features.title: Browser-based SQL client
features.description: Query and manage databases entirely from a web browser, with no desktop installation required.

features.title: Broad database support
features.description: Connects to 100+ SQL, NoSQL, and cloud database types, inherited from the DBeaver engine.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs as a self-hosted web server via Docker Compose, deployable on-premises or in the cloud.

features.title: Enterprise authentication
features.description: Enterprise edition adds SSO (AWS, GCP, Okta), Kerberos, and multi-factor authentication for secure team access.

features.title: Team access management
features.description: Centralized administration of who can access which database connections.

pros: Free, open-source Community Edition available
No desktop client installation needed for end users
Supports the same broad range of databases as desktop DBeaver
Self-hosted, so data stays on your own infrastructure
cons: Community Edition has fewer features than the desktop DBeaver client
Enterprise features like SSO and MFA require a paid license ($205/user/year)
Requires running and maintaining a Docker-based server

faqs.question: Is CloudBeaver free?
faqs.answer: Yes, CloudBeaver Community Edition is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license. A paid Enterprise edition with added security features costs $205 per user per year.

faqs.question: Do I need to install anything to use CloudBeaver?
faqs.answer: End users only need a web browser. The CloudBeaver server itself must be deployed by an administrator, typically via Docker.

faqs.question: How is CloudBeaver different from DBeaver?
faqs.answer: DBeaver is a desktop application installed on each user's machine; CloudBeaver is a self-hosted web server that provides browser-based database access for a team.

use_cases.title: Shared team database access
use_cases.description: Organizations deploy CloudBeaver so analysts and developers can query databases from any browser without local installs.

use_cases.title: Secure enterprise database access
use_cases.description: Enterprises use CloudBeaver Enterprise with SSO and MFA to control and audit who can access production databases.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: CloudBeaver is a free, open-source, self-hosted web SQL client from the DBeaver team, with a paid Enterprise edition for team access controls.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source data platform forked from Directus 9, generating REST and GraphQL APIs from any SQL database.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: d9 is an open-source data platform maintained by La Webcapsule as a continuation of Directus 9, kept GPLv3 licensed after later Directus versions moved to a premium open-source model. It layers REST and GraphQL APIs on top of any SQL database.

d9 shares the same database schema as Directus 9, so existing Directus 9 projects can adopt it as a drop-in replacement without a data migration, while retaining a no-code admin interface, file asset management, and granular permissions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: d9 is built for developers and teams who were using Directus 9 and want to keep a fully open-source, self-hosted data platform without moving to a paid licensing tier, as well as anyone who wants a no-code layer over an existing SQL database.

features.title: Auto-generated REST & GraphQL API
features.description: Instantly exposes a Node.js REST and GraphQL API layer on top of any connected SQL database.

features.title: Multi-database support
features.description: Works with PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, OracleDB, CockroachDB, MariaDB, and MS-SQL.

features.title: No-code admin interface
features.description: Non-technical users can manage data and content through a visual admin panel with role-based permissions.

features.title: Directus 9 compatibility
features.description: Uses the same database schema as Directus 9 for drop-in migration from existing Directus 9 projects.

features.title: Extensible architecture
features.description: Modular design supports custom extensions for developers who need to add functionality.

features.title: No vendor lock-in
features.description: Direct SQL database access means the platform can be removed at any time without losing data.

pros: Completely free and open source under GPLv3, with no premium feature gates
Drop-in compatible with existing Directus 9 databases
Supports both a no-code interface and a full developer API
No artificial limits on users, roles, or records
cons: Maintained by a small independent team rather than a funded company
Based on the older Directus 9 codebase, so it lacks newer Directus features from later major versions
Self-hosting requires the team to manage infrastructure and updates themselves

faqs.question: Is d9 the same as Directus?
faqs.answer: d9 is an independently maintained open-source fork of Directus 9, created after later Directus versions adopted a premium licensing model. It uses the same database schema as Directus 9.

faqs.question: Is d9 free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, d9 is completely free and open source under the GPLv3 license, with no paid tiers or feature gates.

faqs.question: Can I migrate an existing Directus 9 project to d9?
faqs.answer: Yes, because d9 uses the same database schema as Directus 9, existing projects can generally be migrated as a drop-in replacement.

use_cases.title: Headless CMS / backend for apps
use_cases.description: Developers use d9 to instantly generate REST and GraphQL APIs from an existing SQL database for web or mobile apps.

use_cases.title: Staying on open-source Directus
use_cases.description: Teams that adopted Directus 9 use d9 to keep a fully open-source stack instead of upgrading to a paid Directus tier.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: d9 is a free, open-source data platform forked from Directus 9 that generates REST and GraphQL APIs from any SQL database, GPLv3 licensed.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted secure vault that tokenizes and encrypts PII, PHI, and payment data for compliance.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free (open source), with optional paid Pro support

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Databunker is a Go-based, self-hosted secure vault for storing and tokenizing personally identifiable information (PII), protected health information (PHI), payment data, and other sensitive records. Application databases store safe random tokens instead of raw sensitive data.

The core project, maintained by Security Bunker, is free and open source under the MIT license. The company also offers a paid Pro edition and a bundled 'Data & File Lockdown Sprint' engagement combining software with implementation support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Databunker is aimed at engineering and security teams — from early-stage startups to larger enterprises — that need to centralize PII handling, reduce breach exposure, and support compliance work such as GDPR data subject access requests.

features.title: Tokenization engine
features.description: Replaces sensitive fields with random tokens so application databases never store raw PII directly.

features.title: Encrypted PII vault
features.description: Stores encrypted personal records, payment data, and sensitive files with access-controlled retrieval.

features.title: Fuzzy search
features.description: Search encrypted records by approximate matches, such as misspelled names.

features.title: Record versioning and audit logs
features.description: Tracks changes to records and logs access for compliance and audit purposes.

features.title: Key rotation and BYOK
features.description: Supports bring-your-own-key encryption with AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, and HashiCorp Vault.

features.title: Multi-tenancy
features.description: Database-enforced tenant isolation for platforms serving multiple customers or business units.

pros: Free, open-source core under the permissive MIT license
Self-hosted via Docker Compose, Kubernetes, or OpenShift for full data control
Purpose-built for GDPR-style compliance workflows like DSARs
Handles large-scale production use (reported 20M+ records)
cons: Requires engineering effort to integrate into existing applications
Advanced support and implementation help are paid, separate from the free core
Smaller community than general-purpose database or security tools

faqs.question: Is Databunker free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Databunker project is free and open source under the MIT license and can be self-hosted at no cost. A paid Pro edition and implementation support package are also available.

faqs.question: What kind of data does Databunker protect?
faqs.answer: Databunker is designed to store and tokenize PII, PHI, payment data (PCI), and KYC records, keeping raw sensitive data out of application databases.

faqs.question: Can Databunker help with GDPR requests?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes features aimed at fulfilling Data Subject Access Requests (DSARs) and supporting broader GDPR, SOC 2, and ISO 27001 compliance efforts.

use_cases.title: Centralizing PII storage
use_cases.description: Engineering teams route sensitive user fields through Databunker so application databases only ever see tokens.

use_cases.title: GDPR/DSAR compliance
use_cases.description: Privacy teams use Databunker's audit trails and access controls to respond to data subject access requests.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Databunker is an open-source, self-hosted vault that tokenizes and encrypts PII, PHI, and payment data to support privacy compliance.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source tool by Simon Willison for exploring, querying, and publishing data as interactive websites and APIs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Datasette is an open-source tool for exploring and publishing data. It takes data from CSVs, JSON files, or SQLite databases and turns it into a browsable website with a built-in JSON API, letting anyone query, filter, and share findings without writing backend code.

Created by Simon Willison, Datasette is released under the Apache 2.0 license and can be self-hosted or published to platforms like Google Cloud Run, Heroku, or Vercel using the built-in `datasette publish` command. It has grown into an ecosystem of dozens of companion tools and over a hundred plugins.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Datasette is popular with data journalists, researchers, museums, archivists, and local governments who need to publish structured datasets publicly, as well as developers who want a fast JSON API over a dataset without building custom backend infrastructure.

features.title: Instant data exploration
features.description: Import CSV, JSON, or SQLite data and immediately browse, filter, and query it through a web UI.

features.title: Automatic JSON API
features.description: Every table and query is automatically exposed as a JSON API endpoint, with no backend code required.

features.title: One-command publishing
features.description: The `datasette publish` command deploys a dataset directly to Google Cloud Run, Heroku, or Vercel.

features.title: Stored queries
features.description: Save and share custom SQL queries as bookmarkable pages within the published site.

features.title: Plugin ecosystem
features.description: Over 100 plugins extend Datasette with features like authentication, geospatial support, and visualization.

features.title: Row editing
features.description: Optional write support allows editing rows directly through the interface for permitted users.

pros: Completely free and open source (Apache 2.0 license)
Extremely fast way to turn a dataset into a public, queryable website
Large plugin ecosystem for extending functionality
Backed by an active, well-known maintainer and community
cons: Best suited to SQLite-based datasets rather than large live production databases
Requires familiarity with SQL to get the most out of custom queries
No official managed hosting; publishing relies on third-party cloud platforms

faqs.question: Is Datasette free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Datasette is completely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: Who created Datasette?
faqs.answer: Datasette was created by Simon Willison and is maintained as an active open-source project with a wider ecosystem of plugins and companion tools.

faqs.question: Can I publish a dataset publicly with Datasette?
faqs.answer: Yes, the `datasette publish` command can deploy a dataset directly to services like Google Cloud Run, Heroku, or Vercel as a public website and API.

use_cases.title: Publishing public datasets
use_cases.description: Journalists and government agencies publish CSV or database exports as searchable, linkable websites.

use_cases.title: Rapid API prototyping
use_cases.description: Developers spin up a queryable JSON API over a dataset without writing backend code.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Datasette is a free, open-source tool by Simon Willison that turns CSV, JSON, or SQLite data into browsable websites with a built-in JSON API.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, PHP-based low-code database framework for building custom business applications with drag-and-drop tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Limbas is an open-source, low-code database framework for building database-driven business applications. It provides drag-and-drop tools for generating tables, forms, reports, and queries without hand-coding a full application.

The Limbas framework is licensed under the GNU GPL v2.0, with no license fees for the core software. It can work with multiple database backends including PostgreSQL, MySQL, MS SQL Server, SAP MaxDB, and Hana DB, and includes flexible group- and user-level permissions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Limbas is aimed at organizations and developers who want to build internal business applications — such as workflow tools, record management, or simple document management systems — on top of a relational database without starting from scratch.

features.title: Drag-and-drop table design
features.description: Generate and link database tables and fields visually instead of writing SQL schema by hand.

features.title: Form and report generator
features.description: Build data entry forms and reports through a visual designer.

features.title: Graphical query builder
features.description: Construct database queries without writing raw SQL.

features.title: Workflow engine
features.description: Define business process workflows on top of stored data.

features.title: Built-in document management
features.description: Includes basic DMS functionality alongside the core database tooling.

features.title: Granular rights management
features.description: Assign permissions flexibly by table, field, and record through user and group management.

pros: Free and open source under GPL v2.0 with no license fees
Supports multiple relational database backends
Low-code approach speeds up building internal business apps
Includes workflow and document management beyond basic CRUD
cons: Smaller community and ecosystem compared to mainstream low-code platforms
PHP-based architecture may feel dated compared to newer no-code tools
Limited English-language documentation and support outside German-speaking markets

faqs.question: Is Limbas free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Limbas framework is licensed under GNU GPL v2.0 and is free to use with no license fees.

faqs.question: What databases does Limbas support?
faqs.answer: Limbas can work with PostgreSQL, MySQL, MS SQL Server, SAP MaxDB, and SAP Hana DB, among others.

faqs.question: Does Limbas require coding?
faqs.answer: Limbas is a low-code framework — tables, forms, reports, and queries can be built through drag-and-drop tools, though custom logic may still require development.

use_cases.title: Internal business applications
use_cases.description: Organizations build custom record-management or workflow apps on Limbas instead of using spreadsheets.

use_cases.title: Lightweight document management
use_cases.description: Teams use Limbas's built-in DMS features alongside its database tooling to manage business documents.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Limbas is a free, open-source, low-code database framework for building custom business applications with drag-and-drop tools.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source network-wide DNS server that blocks ads and trackers for every device on your network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: AdGuard Home is a network-wide DNS server that blocks ads, trackers, and malicious domains for every device on a network, without needing per-device apps or browser extensions. It's built and maintained by AdGuard.

It is self-hosted software, deployable on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Raspberry Pi, with official Docker images, an OpenWrt LuCI app, and a Home Assistant add-on, giving it broad options for running on home routers, NAS devices, and small servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: AdGuard Home is aimed at home users and self-hosters who want to block ads and trackers across every device on their network at once — including IoT devices and smart TVs that can't run individual ad-blocking apps.

features.title: Network-wide ad and tracker blocking
features.description: Blocks ads and tracking domains at the DNS level for all connected devices, not just browsers.

features.title: Parental controls
features.description: Enforce Safe Search and content filtering for children's devices across the network.

features.title: Custom filter lists
features.description: Use community ad-block filter lists or create custom blocking rules.

features.title: IoT protection
features.description: Protects smart-home and IoT devices that don't support individual ad blockers or VPN clients.

features.title: Network activity dashboard
features.description: View query logs and statistics on DNS activity across the network.

features.title: Multi-platform deployment
features.description: Runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, Raspberry Pi, Docker, and OpenWrt routers.

pros: Completely free and open source
Blocks ads for every device on the network, including IoT devices
Runs on inexpensive hardware like a Raspberry Pi
Includes parental control and Safe Search enforcement
cons: DNS-level blocking can miss ads embedded within apps that bypass system DNS
Requires some networking knowledge to configure as the network's DNS resolver
Self-hosted, so uptime and updates are the user's responsibility

faqs.question: Is AdGuard Home free?
faqs.answer: Yes, AdGuard Home is free, open-source, self-hosted software.

faqs.question: What platforms does AdGuard Home run on?
faqs.answer: AdGuard Home runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux, including Raspberry Pi, and is also available via Docker, OpenWrt, and Home Assistant.

faqs.question: How is AdGuard Home different from browser ad blockers?
faqs.answer: AdGuard Home blocks ads and trackers at the DNS level for every device on the network, whereas browser extensions only block ads within that one browser.

use_cases.title: Whole-home ad blocking
use_cases.description: Households run AdGuard Home on a Raspberry Pi or home server to block ads across all devices at once.

use_cases.title: Protecting IoT and smart-home devices
use_cases.description: Self-hosters use AdGuard Home to filter DNS traffic from smart TVs and IoT gadgets that can't run their own ad blockers.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: AdGuard Home is a free, open-source, self-hosted DNS server that blocks ads and trackers for every device on your home network.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source DNS proxy and ad-blocker written in Go, with allowlisting, caching, and DoH/DoT support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: blocky is a fast, lightweight DNS proxy and ad-blocker written in Go, designed to block ads and malware domains for a local network while also improving DNS performance through caching and prefetching.

It is developed as a spare-time open-source project with no telemetry or data collection, and is distributed as a single binary or Docker image, making it easy to run on hardware like Raspberry Pi or OpenWrt routers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: blocky is built for network administrators, homelab users, and privacy-conscious households who want fine-grained control over DNS-level blocking and forwarding, including per-client-group policies.

features.title: Ad and malware blocking
features.description: Blocks DNS queries using external block lists with regex support and per-client-group allowlisting.

features.title: Modern DNS protocol support
features.description: Handles DNS over UDP/TCP, DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH), DNS-over-TLS (DoT), DoQ, and DoH3.

features.title: DNS caching and prefetching
features.description: Caches responses and prefetches popular domains to speed up DNS resolution.

features.title: Conditional forwarding
features.description: Routes queries for specific domains to different upstream resolvers.

features.title: DNSSEC validation
features.description: Validates DNS responses using DNSSEC for added security.

features.title: REST API and Prometheus metrics
features.description: Exposes a REST API (with Swagger docs) to control blocking status, plus a Prometheus metrics endpoint for monitoring.

pros: Completely free, open source, with explicitly no telemetry
Single-binary deployment makes it easy to run on low-power hardware
Supports modern encrypted DNS protocols (DoH, DoT, DoQ, DoH3)
REST API and Prometheus metrics support monitoring and automation
cons: Configuration is file/YAML-based, which is less approachable than a GUI-first tool
Maintained as a spare-time project by an individual developer rather than a company
No built-in web dashboard as polished as some competing DNS blockers

faqs.question: Is blocky free?
faqs.answer: Yes, blocky is free and open source, developed with no telemetry or data collection.

faqs.question: Does blocky support encrypted DNS?
faqs.answer: Yes, blocky supports DNS over UDP/TCP as well as encrypted protocols including DoH, DoT, DoQ, and DoH3.

faqs.question: Can I monitor blocky with Prometheus?
faqs.answer: Yes, blocky can export metrics for Prometheus and includes an example Grafana dashboard definition.

use_cases.title: Homelab DNS-level blocking
use_cases.description: Self-hosters run blocky as their network's DNS resolver to block ads and malware for all devices.

use_cases.title: Router-based ad blocking
use_cases.description: Users deploy blocky directly on OpenWrt routers or Raspberry Pi for whole-network filtering.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: blocky is a free, open-source DNS proxy and ad-blocker written in Go, with allowlisting, caching, and encrypted DNS protocol support.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source local ad blocker for macOS and Linux that runs natively on your device, no server required.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Maza is a local ad blocker created by developer Andros Fenollosa, described as being like Pi-hole but running natively on your own operating system instead of requiring a separate server or network-wide DNS setup.
It is open source and free, distributed via GitHub, and supports macOS and Linux, giving individual users a lightweight way to block ads without dedicated hardware.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Maza is aimed at individual macOS and Linux users who want simple, device-level ad blocking without setting up a separate DNS server or router-level solution like Pi-hole.

features.title: Native local ad blocking
features.description: Blocks ads directly on your macOS or Linux machine rather than through a separate network appliance.

features.title: No server required
features.description: Unlike Pi-hole, Maza doesn't need a dedicated device or network-wide DNS configuration to run.

features.title: Lightweight and simple
features.description: Focused on straightforward, efficient ad blocking without a complex setup process.

features.title: Open-source distribution
features.description: Source code and installation instructions are available on GitHub.

pros: Free and open source
Simpler setup than a network-wide solution like Pi-hole
Runs natively without extra hardware or a dedicated server
Lightweight, focused tool
cons: Only protects the single device it's installed on, not the whole network
Limited to macOS and Linux, with no Windows support
Smaller community and feature set compared to established tools like Pi-hole

faqs.question: Is Maza free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Maza is free and open source, distributed via GitHub.

faqs.question: What platforms does Maza support?
faqs.answer: Maza supports macOS and Linux.

faqs.question: How is Maza different from Pi-hole?
faqs.answer: Pi-hole blocks ads network-wide via a dedicated DNS server, while Maza runs locally on an individual macOS or Linux machine without needing separate server hardware.

use_cases.title: Single-device ad blocking
use_cases.description: Individual users install Maza to block ads on their personal macOS or Linux computer.

use_cases.title: Simple alternative to Pi-hole
use_cases.description: Users who don't want to manage a separate Pi-hole server use Maza for lightweight local blocking instead.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Maza is a free, open-source local ad blocker for macOS and Linux that runs natively on your device without a separate server.
